summary: Charity Majors pushes back against the 'Observability 3.0' framing proposed by Hazel Weakly, arguing that the critical advances described are already part of the Observability 2.0 generational shift already underway. She contends that the real measure of success isn't non-engineering functions interrogating engineering data, but rather engineers learning to integrate business data into their own telemetry and describe their work in the language of the business.
key_points: Semantic versioning as a framing device is overused and easily co-opted, but Observability 2.0 describes a real, backwards-incompatible generational shift from three pillars to wide structured events
New observability startups have stopped looking like cheaper Datadogs and started looking like cheaper Honeycombs, validating the architectural shift
Good product marketing simplifies strong technical differentiators; bad product marketing starts with justification and works backwards
Engineering is the laggard compared to business functions that have had columnar stores and flexible querying for decades
The end of ZIRP is forcing engineering leaders to justify their decisions in business terms, which requires integrating business data into engineering telemetry
money_quotes: This is not a branding exercise in search of technological justification, it's a technical sea change reaching for clarification in the market.
The success of observability 2.0 will be measured by how well engineering teams can understand their decisions and describe what they do in the language of the business.

It's a very 'the cobbler's children have no shoes' kind of vibe, that we're still over here warring over cardinality limits and pre-defined metrics and trying to wrestle them into understanding our massively, sprawlingly complex systems.

Twenty years of free money has done poorly by us as engineering leaders. The end of the ZIRP era is the best thing that could have happened to us. It's time to get our house in order and sit at the big table.

Every truly interesting question we ask as engineers is some combination or intersection of business data + application data. We do no one any favors by chopping them up and siloing them off into different tools and data stores, for consumption by different teams.
key_insight: The real observability revolution isn't about non-engineering teams accessing telemetry data — it's about engineering teams finally integrating business context into their own tooling so they can speak the language of the business and earn a seat at the executive table.
